

Policy decomposition is a way to describe *bounded-rationality* and *complex decision-making*
in humans.

::: {.callout-tip title="Definition: Policy Decomposition"}
Human policy $\pi^{.}$ can be decomposed into a *planner-reward* pair $(p, R)$ via $p(R) = \pi^.$. Planner $p$ encodes the (ir)rationality, biases, and style of the human, while reward $R$ describes the human's goal.

[@occam]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}
:::

<div style="display: flex; gap: 1em;">
<div style="flex: 1;">
MIND-MELD accounts for suboptimality by learning to adjust and correct for annotator skill.

[@mindmeld]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}
</div>
<div style="border-left: 1px solid #ccc; margin: 0 0.5em;"></div>
<div style="flex: 1;">
*Inverse reward design* accounts for suboptimality of hand-crafted rewards: adopting risk-averse behavior when needed.

[@ird]{style="font-size: 0.6em;"}
</div>
</div>

::: {.notes}
Another interesting approach is IRD, which examines hand-crafted rewards. Here they develop an approach to use risk-averse behavior to stay in regions that the hand-crafted reward describes well.

Another way the planner reward decomposition is helpful is in heterogeneity...
:::
